title: Spinal Cord Compression
objectives: Recognize clinical presentation of cord compression
Identify sensory level and its significance
Understand urgency of diagnosis and treatment
Know indications for emergent MRI and steroids

vignette.text:
A 68-year-old man with history of prostate cancer (diagnosed 2 years ago) presents with 1 week of progressive back pain and 2 days of leg weakness. Today he noticed difficulty urinating. The pain is worse at night and with lying flat.

Past Medical History: Prostate cancer (Gleason 8), treated with radiation

physicalExam.general: Uncomfortable, cannot lie flat due to pain
physicalExam.neurologic: Bilateral lower extremity weakness (3/5), decreased sensation below T10, absent ankle reflexes, decreased rectal tone

questions.question: What triad suggests spinal cord compression?
questions.explanation: Classic triad: back pain (often worse at night/supine) + progressive weakness + bladder/bowel dysfunction. In cancer patients with back pain, always consider cord compression.

questions.question: What does the T10 sensory level indicate?
questions.explanation: Sensory level indicates the level of cord involvement (lesion at or above that level). T10 = umbilicus level. The sensory level helps localize the lesion for imaging.

questions.question: What is the diagnostic study of choice?
questions.explanation: MRI of ENTIRE spine with contrast is gold standard. Must image entire spine as there may be multiple levels of disease (especially in metastatic cancer). Time is critical - ambulation at diagnosis predicts ambulation after treatment.

questions.question: What is the role of dexamethasone?
options: Reduces edema and preserves neurologic function
questions.explanation: High-dose dexamethasone (10mg IV then 4mg q6h) reduces vasogenic edema around the tumor, potentially preserving neurologic function. Give immediately if cord compression suspected (don't wait for MRI).

keyTakeaways: Triad: back pain (worse supine) + weakness + bladder/bowel dysfunction
MRI entire spine with contrast - don't delay
Dexamethasone immediately if suspected (don't wait for MRI)
Prognosis depends on neuro function at diagnosis - TIME IS FUNCTION
